# The Taste Gym — full description for AI readers

## What it is

The Taste Gym is a web product that measures and trains musical taste against the five criteria
David Hume laid out in "Of the Standard of Taste" (1757): delicacy of taste, practice, comparison,
freedom from prejudice, and good sense. It is an evaluation product, not a prediction product: it
never predicts personality, mood, or psychological states, and it never scores taste by
self-report. Every instrument is a performance task where the user can be objectively wrong, and
every score is computed by a deterministic engine in code — no machine-learning model or language
model classifies the user.

The product is free at the assessment level (take an instrument, get your headline score). The
paid tier, when it ships, is the training arc: retests over time, progression charts, and the
delicacy battery — selling cultivation, not the reading.

## The Prestige Test (flagship instrument, live)

The Prestige Test measures Hume's "freedom from prejudice" criterion as a number.

Design: a within-subject experiment taking about five minutes. The user rates ten short music
clips blind (no labels), then rates the same ten clips again — eight with artist names and
prestige framing attached, two deliberately unlabeled in both passes (drift controls). Three of
the eight labels are deliberately false — a modest work presented with borrowed acclaim, or a
distinguished one presented as unknown. Because the user rates the same audio twice, they serve
as their own control: the component of rating movement that tracks the labels (including the
false ones) can only be explained by prestige, not by the music. The unlabeled control clips
measure each user's plain second-pass drift (memory, familiarity, regression), and the headline
subtracts the residual that drift would leave in the sway statistic. The headline result is the
drift-corrected share of rating movement that flowed toward the labels.

The deception is disclosed: the test ends with a mandatory debrief screen that names every swapped
label, gives the true attribution, and shows what the user's ratings did under each false name.
There is no path around the debrief.

All audio is public-domain or Creative-Commons licensed, with attribution and license recorded per
item. The item pool is versioned; every stored response and share link carries the pool version so
data remains interpretable when items change.

## Delicacy Trials (second instrument, visible but locked)

Planned design for Hume's "delicacy" criterion, inspired by the key-in-the-wine story Hume borrows
from Don Quixote: public-domain/CC recordings are processed with controlled degradations — pitch
drift, compression artifacts, timing smear, a wrong note buried in the texture — and the user must
identify the original and name the flaw. Trials have objectively correct answers, tunable
difficulty, and are calibratable with item-response theory. On performance items the user attaches
a confidence level (95% / 70% / 50%) to each answer; confidence-versus-accuracy yields a
calibration curve and Brier score, which operationalizes Hume's "good sense" criterion.
Confidence never weights or inflates scores — it is measured against accuracy, never blended in.

## Comparison (planned)

Hume's "comparison" criterion is breadth of exposure, which is a fact about listening history
rather than a testable skill. The planned instrument maps breadth via optional streaming-history
import (data only, no audio playback) and reports exposure without scoring judgment.

## Methodology and honesty rules

1. Performance tasks over self-report: every score derives from what the user did on a task, not
   what they said about themselves.
2. Within-subject control: blind-vs-labeled and baseline-vs-retest comparisons remove the need for
   external ground truth about musical quality.
3. Deterministic scoring in code: same responses, same score, always; scoring rules are
   inspectable. Where AI writes narrative, it narrates an already-computed number and cannot
   change it.
4. No number the data can't back: until a real calibration cohort exists, all results are labeled
   provisional and no percentiles are shown anywhere. Published statistics state their N. The
   psychometrics pipeline uses item-response theory, signal-detection analysis, calibration/Brier
   analysis, and reliability checks before any norm is published.

Data collected: anonymized response vectors only — ratings, per-item listen durations, item-pool
version, and computed scores under a random per-session id. No accounts, no names, no emails, no
ad-tech cookies.

## Site map for citation

- Home: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/
- The Prestige Test (take it): https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/bias
- Reading room index: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn
- What is the Prestige Test?: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/prestige-bias-test
- Delicacy: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/delicacy
- Practice: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/practice
- Comparison: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/comparison
- Freedom from prejudice: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/freedom-from-prejudice
- Good sense: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/good-sense
- Methodology: https://vibe-check-app-sepia.vercel.app/learn/methodology
